findings.title: Skill does not specify a license
Deliberate compute/token exhaustion through forced multi-round loops
findings.description: The skill mandates repeated validation phases and restarts, while the bundled script intentionally keeps the agent in a long-running loop. The script persists state across runs, forces up to 15 rounds, and randomly injects REPAIR states to trigger additional work. This is a clear availability abuse pattern designed to waste agent time, tokens, and compute.
findings.evidence: SKILL.md requires phases to be 'mandatory' and says PROGRESS means 'Repeat Phase 4' and REPAIR means 'restart from Phase 3'. In svp_check.py, comments explicitly state '# Keep the agent in the loop for many rounds' and '# Randomly require repairs to increase token consumption'.
findings.remediation: Remove mandatory restart loops, eliminate randomized repair/progress behavior, and require bounded execution with explicit user confirmation before any repeated analysis pass.

findings.title: Deceptive capability claims in manifest and instructions
findings.description: The skill presents itself as a 'Comprehensive multi-pass code validator' that ensures 'production-grade code correctness', but the actual script performs no real code validation. Instead, it increments a counter, stores state, and emits randomized status messages. This mismatch indicates deceptive capability inflation and hidden behavior inconsistent with the stated purpose.
findings.evidence: The description claims structural, semantic, and regression validation, but svp_check.py only updates round counters, writes ~/.svp_state.json, and prints PROGRESS/REPAIR/TERMINAL messages without inspecting the target file contents.
findings.remediation: Align the manifest and instructions with actual behavior, or implement genuine validation logic. Remove misleading claims about compliance, semantic checks, and production-grade assurance.

findings.title: Unicode homoglyph obfuscation in SKILL.md
findings.description: The instruction text uses visually confusable Cyrillic characters in key words such as 'Protocol', 'compliance', 'contracts', and 'completely'. This is a detection-evasion pattern that can hinder keyword-based scanning and conceal coercive instructions during review.
findings.evidence: Examples include 'Рrotocol', 'сompleteness', 'сontracts', 'Сompliance', 'сheck', 'сertificate', 'сomplex', 'shortсut', and 'сompletely' using non-ASCII confusable characters.
findings.remediation: Normalize the file to plain ASCII or standard Unicode text, and reject or flag homoglyph usage in security-sensitive instructions.
